complete_examples.overview: Complete examples of Debug mode across all major scenarios.

example.name: single_test_failure_hands_off
example.scenario: Debug is spawned to investigate a unit test that keeps failing after multiple auto-fix attempts. The test asserts that a discount calculation returns 20% off for premium users, but it's returning 10%. hands_off_mode is true.
example.spawn_prompt: Failing test: DiscountService.test.ts — "applies 20% discount for premium users" Expected: 80 (20% off 100), Received: 90 (10% off 100) What was tried: (1) Checked DiscountService.getDiscountRate — looked correct. (2) Checked UserService.isPremium — returns correct boolean. (3) Checked for caching issues — no cache involved. hands_off_mode: true

step.approach: Read DiscountService.getDiscountRate. The method has a switch on user tier: "premium" → 0.20, "gold" → 0.15, "standard" → 0.10. Read UserService.isPremium. It calls user.tier === "premium". Read the user data source. The database stores tier as "Premium" (capital P). The comparison is case-sensitive — user.tier is "Premium" but the check is for "premium". Root cause is clear from code analysis alone.
Fix the case sensitivity: change isPremium to compare user.tier.toLowerCase() === "premium". (Or fix the data source — but the simplest fix with least blast radius is the comparison.)
Run unit + integration tests. All pass. (Not an E2E fix — skip E2E.)
Tests passing = verified. hands_off_mode is true — no user interaction.
Write a unit test: "should apply correct discount for case-insensitive tier matching". Test with "premium", "Premium", "PREMIUM" — all should get 20%.
Run unit + integration + new regression test. All pass. Report success.

example.completion: Root cause: case-sensitive string comparison in UserService.isPremium ("Premium" !== "premium"). Fix: case-insensitive comparison. Regression test: tests all case variants. All tests green. No instrumentation needed — root cause was clear from code analysis.

example.name: single_test_failure_requires_instrumentation
example.scenario: Debug is spawned for a test failure where code analysis alone is inconclusive. The test sometimes passes and sometimes fails — a race condition.
example.spawn_prompt: Failing test (flaky): OrderProcessor.test.ts — "processes orders in correct sequence" Sometimes passes (about 60% of the time), sometimes fails with wrong order. What was tried: (1) Checked array.sort — looks correct. (2) Added artificial delays — didn't change pass rate. hands_off_mode: true

step.approach: Read OrderProcessor.processOrders. It uses Promise.all to process orders in parallel and pushes results to an array. Promise.all preserves order of the input array, but the processing function uses an external queue that may reorder items. Suspect: race condition in the async queue. But without runtime evidence, can't be sure. Evidence is inconclusive → must instrument.

Add [DEBUG] logs to trace: order ID, enqueue time, dequeue time, processing start, completion. Re-run the test 10 times autonomously. In the failing runs: order #3 is dequeued before order #2. Root cause: the queue implementation uses a Map with numeric keys but iterates in insertion order, and a concurrent dequeue during insertion can skip entries.
Replace Map-based queue with an array-based queue (shift/push) with a mutex lock to prevent concurrent modifications.

step.description: Run existing tests, verify, regression test, final suite
step.approach: Run tests — all pass (including 20 re-runs of the flaky test). Write regression test: runs the order processing 100 times and asserts correct sequence every time. Run full suite — all green. Remove all [DEBUG] statements. Report success.

example.completion: Root cause: race condition in Map-based async queue allowing skipped entries during concurrent dequeue. Fix: array-based queue with mutex. Regression test: 100-iteration sequencing stress test. Instrumentation was essential — code analysis alone couldn't confirm the race condition.

example.name: smoke_test_failure_interactive
example.scenario: Debug is spawned to investigate a failed smoke test. The user reports: "The dashboard widgets don't stack vertically when I resize to 400px — they overflow horizontally." hands_off_mode is false.
example.spawn_prompt: Smoke test failure: Dashboard responsive layout — widgets should stack vertically at 400px width. User reports: widgets overflow horizontally instead of stacking. Plan context provided (dashboard feature, CSS grid layout). hands_off_mode: false smoke_test_classification: HUMAN/SHT REQUIRED

step.approach: Read Dashboard.css. The grid uses: grid-template-columns: 1fr 300px. At 400px viewport, the 300px sidebar + 1fr content doesn't leave room. Suspect: missing minmax(0, 1fr) on the content column — without it, 1fr can't shrink below its content's minimum size. But this is a visual layout issue — need to confirm with the user. Root cause is plausible from code but not certain → instrument to confirm.

Add [DEBUG] logs to the Dashboard component that log: - Computed grid column widths at render - Viewport width - Content column min-width Tell user: "I've added diagnostic logs. Open DevTools Console (F12), resize to 400px, and copy the [DEBUG] output back to me."

User copies: "[DEBUG] Viewport: 400px, Grid columns: [100px, 300px], Content min-width: 350px" Confirmed: the content column has a 350px min-width from its child content, preventing the grid from shrinking it below 350px. The sidebar is 300px. Total needed: 650px > 400px viewport.
Change grid-template-columns to: minmax(0, 1fr) 300px. Add a media query: at max-width 600px, switch to single column: grid-template-columns: 1fr.
Run unit + integration tests. All pass.

ask_followup_question: "Fix applied — grid now uses minmax(0, 1fr) with a single-column breakpoint at 600px. Resize to 400px — do widgets stack vertically now?" User confirms: "Yes, it works perfectly."

step.description: Write regression test + run all tests
step.approach: Write an integration test that renders the dashboard at 400px viewport width and asserts the computed grid is single-column. Run all tests — green. Remove [DEBUG] statements. Report success.

example.completion: Root cause: CSS grid missing minmax(0, 1fr) preventing content column from shrinking below child min-width. Fix: minmax(0, 1fr) + responsive breakpoint. Regression test: viewport-width-based layout assertion. User confirmed fix works. All tests green.

example.name: multi_issue_interactive
example.scenario: User invokes Debug directly with 3 bugs found during manual testing of a todo app. hands_off_mode is false (default).
example.user_request: I found 3 bugs: 1. Adding a todo with an empty title creates a blank todo item 2. Completed todos show the wrong timestamp (shows creation time instead of completion time) 3. Deleting the last todo in a list crashes the app

step.approach: Analyze all 3 bugs. Bug #3 (crash) is most critical. Bug #1 (validation) is simplest. Bug #2 (timestamp) has no dependency on either. Order: #1 (simplest first, build momentum) → #3 (needs #1's validation to test edge case) → #2 (independent). Present to user: "I'll fix in this order: empty title validation → delete-last crash → timestamp. OK?" User confirms.

Read TodoInput.tsx. The addTodo function doesn't validate title length. Fix: add validation — if title.trim() is empty, show error "Title cannot be empty." Run unit + integration tests → all pass. Verify with user → confirmed.

Read TodoList.tsx. The deleteTodo function accesses list[list.length - 1] after splice — when the list becomes empty, this is undefined and a downstream .id access throws. Fix: add early return if list becomes empty after deletion. Run unit + integration tests → all pass. Verify with user → confirmed.

Read TodoItem.tsx. The completedAt is set to todo.createdAt instead of new Date(). Fix: set completedAt to new Date().toISOString() when toggling to completed. Run unit + integration tests → all pass. Verify with user → confirmed.
"All 3 bugs fixed. Please verify everything works: add a todo (try empty title), complete it (check timestamp), delete all todos (check no crash). Everything good?" User confirms all good.

Write 3 regression tests: 1. should_show_error_when_adding_empty_todo 2. should_not_crash_when_deleting_last_todo 3. should_set_completedAt_to_current_time_when_toggling_done Run all regression tests → pass. Run full suite → all green.

example.completion: 3 bugs fixed: empty title validation, delete-last crash guard, completion timestamp fix. 3 regression tests written. All tests green. User confirmed everything works.

example.name: e2e_regression_hands_off
example.scenario: Debug is spawned to investigate a failing E2E test. The E2E test: "user can complete checkout flow" — fails at the payment step with a 500 error from the payment API. hands_off_mode is true.
example.spawn_prompt: Failing E2E: checkout-flow.spec.ts — "completes checkout with valid payment" Fails at step: "fills payment form and clicks Pay Now" — server returns 500. Stack trace shows PaymentService.processPayment throws. e2e-authoring-guide.md available (path provided by spawner). hands_off_mode: true "Never ask questions. Fix until E2E passes."

step.approach: Read PaymentService.processPayment. It calls externalPaymentClient.charge(). The error handling catches exceptions but re-throws as generic 500. Read externalPaymentClient — it expects amount in cents (integer) but receives dollars (float). When the amount is 29.99 (dollars), it sends 29.99 to the API which expects 2999 (cents). Root cause is clear from code analysis: unit mismatch.
Fix PaymentService.processPayment to convert dollars to cents: Math.round(amount * 100). Do NOT modify the E2E test.
Run unit + integration tests. All pass.
hands_off_mode is true — no user interaction.
Write a unit test: "should convert dollar amounts to cents before calling payment client". Test with various amounts: 29.99 → 2999, 10.00 → 1000, 0.99 → 99.
Run unit + integration + regression test + full E2E suite. All pass including the checkout E2E. Report success.

example.completion: Root cause: PaymentService sent dollar amounts to a cents-based payment API. Fix: dollar-to-cents conversion. Regression test: unit test verifying conversion. E2E suite green. E2E test NOT modified — source code was the problem.

example.name: retry_with_mandatory_instrumentation
example.scenario: Debug skips instrumentation on first pass (root cause seemed clear from code). Fix is applied and verified with user — but user says it's still broken. Second pass MUST include instrumentation.

step.description: First pass: code analysis → fix → fails
step.approach: User reports: "Search returns no results for partial matches." Code analysis: SearchService.query uses exact string matching (===) instead of includes(). Fix: change === to .includes(). Run tests → pass. Ask user to verify. User: "Still broken — I search for 'pro' and 'product' doesn't show up."

Previous fix was based on code analysis alone and failed. Must instrument now. Add [DEBUG] logs to SearchService: - Log the raw query string received - Log the search index being queried - Log each item tested and whether it matched Tell user: "I've added diagnostic logs. Search for 'pro' again and copy the DevTools Console output." User provides: "[DEBUG] Query: 'pro', Index: 'title_index', Items tested: ['Product A' → no match, ...]" The index is the TITLE index, but the user expects full-text search. Real root cause: the search query is being routed to the wrong index (title_index instead of full_text_index).

Fix the index routing: SearchService.query should use full_text_index for partial matches, not title_index. The .includes() change was correct but insufficient — the query wasn't reaching the right data. Run tests → pass. Verify with user → "Perfect, works now!" Write regression test that verifies partial match routing to correct index. Run full suite → green. Remove [DEBUG] statements. Report success.

example.completion: First fix (.includes()) was correct code but insufficient — the real bug was index routing. Mandatory instrumentation on retry revealed the query was hitting the wrong search index. Lesson: a failed code-analysis-only fix means the analysis missed something — instrumentation closes that gap.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Diagnostic-first means analyze evidence FIRST — don't instrument what test output already reveals
Mandatory instrumentation on retry prevents the "guess → fail → guess again" loop
Fix ALL test failures — pre-existing or introduced — leave the project greener
Regression tests after user verification (interactive) or as verification (hands-off)
Multi-issue: triage, fix one-by-one, verify all, batch regression tests
E2E tests are never modified — fix the source code, not the test
Hands-off mode: all investigation autonomous, no user questions ever
Pipeline-agnostic: work the same whether spawned or invoked directly
